source_metadata.title_raw: Michelle Marchetta Kenyon - A-18-223 - November 13, 2018 - Oakland

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 13, 2018

Michelle Marchetta Kenyon
Attorney, Town of Moraga
Burke Williams & Sorensen, LLP
1901 Harrison Street, Suite 900
Oakland, CA 94612-3501

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-223

Dear Ms. Kenyon:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Town of Moraga Mayor Dave Trotter regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090, and advice that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

The Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Accordingly, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any past conduct, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Mayor Trotter from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the Bollinger Valley Project, a residential development project that would allow for up to 126 single-family homes, given that the Project is located within approximately 2,517 feet of the Mayor's residence, and that street construction and improvements necessary for the Project are located within approximately 650 feet of his residence?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in those decisions because they would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest in his residence.

___________________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-223
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are the Town Attorney for the Town of Moraga and the authorized representative of
Mayor Dave Trotter. Moraga is located in a semi-rural setting in southwest Contra Costa County,
east of the Berkeley Hills, south of State Route 24 and west of Interstate 680. Moraga is bordered
by the City of Orinda to the northwest and the City of Lafayette to the north and northeast. Moraga
residents must travel through one of those Cities to go to or from the nearest on and off-ramps for
State Route 24.

The Bollinger Valley Project is a proposed residential development project located in the
eastern portion of Moraga, immediately south of Moraga's border with Lafayette. The Project
would allow for up to 126 detached single-family homes on lots ranging in size from 15,000 to
40,000 square feet. The 186-acre Project Site is owned by the developer of the Project, the
Bruzzone family, and is currently completely comprised of dedicated open space pursuant to the
Moraga General Plan. The Bruzzone family submitted a General Plan Study for the Project to the
Town in 2003, followed by a Conceptual Development and Conservation Plan and a General Plan
Amendment and Rezoning application in 2005.

A Draft Environmental Impact Report ("DEIR") for the Project was issued on February 22,
2013, a Final Environmental Impact Report ("FEIR") was made available in January of 2017, and a
Revised Final Environmental Impact Report was issued in September of 2018. The DEIR indicates
that if the Project were to proceed at its maximum 126-unit capacity, the Project would result in
significant impacts before and after mitigation to air quality and transportation, circulation, and
parking, and significant impacts to noise levels reduced to less than significant by mitigation.

The Project includes the construction of a 5,500-foot emergency vehicle access ("EVA")
road that will connect the Project with St. Mary's Road, one of several major streets running
through Moraga and Lafayette that enables Moraga residents to travel to and from the nearest on
and-off ramps to State Route 24. The EVA will commence within the boundaries of the Project and
extend into four adjoining parcels to the east that are located within Lafayette and owned by the
Bruzzone family. The EVA would only provide emergency access to and from the Project and
would rarely, if ever, be used.

The Mayor owns his residence located approximately 2,517 feet west of the Project. In order
to construct the EVA, minor grading will be necessary approximately 882 feet north of his
residence. Once completed, the EVA will be approximately 933 feet north of his property at its
nearest point and approximately 4,067 feet northeast of the property at its farthest point. The
Mayor's neighborhood is accessed from St. Mary's Road by heading east on Bollinger Canyon
Road and then north on Joseph Drive. The intersection of Bollinger Canyon Road and Joseph Drive
is approximately 1,600 feet from the Mayor's residence.

Bollinger Canyon Road and Valley Hill Drive provide the only ingress and egress for many
existing properties in the area surrounding the Project, including the Mayor's residence. The DEIR
provides as follows with respect to the use of Bollinger Canyon Road and Valley Hill Drive during
construction of the Project:

File No. A-18-223
Page No. 3

Bollinger Canyon Road is narrow and contains blind curves; and existing
intersections with St. Mary's Road, Joseph Drive, and Valley Hill Road [sic]
are dangerous and already overcrowded. ... Bollinger Canyon Road and
Valley Hill Road were not constructed for heavy use, and construction trucks
and increased traffic with the Project may degrade road integrity. ...
Installation of underground utilities along Bollinger Canyon Road and Valley
Hill Road will likely result in closures required for construction trenching. As
the only ingress or egress for many properties, the EIR should analyze how
this construction can occur without disrupting access for the existing residents;
and if the roadway provides adequate emergency access and evacuation.

If the Project is approved and constructed, Project residents would be added to the existing residents
in this eastern portion of Moraga just south of its border with Lafayette, including the Mayor,
whose only ingress or egress is Bollinger Canyon Road and Valley Hill Drive. Construction of the
Project and the installation of underground utilities necessary to support it may affect existing area
residents' ability to access their residences. At its nearest point, Bollinger Canyon Road is
approximately 650 feet southwest of the Mayor's residence.

Among the mitigation measures included in the DEIR and FEIR in response to
transportation impacts resultant from the Project is the installation of a left-turn lane on southbound
St. Mary's Road at its intersection with Bollinger Canyon Road, and the improvement of Bollinger
Canyon Road between Joseph Drive and Valley Hill Drive. At their nearest points, the left-turn lane
on St. Mary's Road would be approximately 650 feet southwest of, and the intersection of Bollinger
Canyon Road and Joseph Drive is approximately 1,600 feet southeast of, the Mayor's residence.

You note that the Mayor's residence's primary viewsheds are directed northwest, away from
the EVA and the Project. The residential portion of the Project will not be visible from the Mayor's
residence. With respect to the EVA, however, some grading will temporarily be visible during
construction from the side yard of the Mayor's residence through intervening trees. After
construction, portions of the completed EVA may also be visible from that perspective. However,
neither the grading nor the completed EVA will be visible from within the Mayor's residence
because of large oak trees obstructing their view.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, participating
in making, or attempting to you his or her office a governmental decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a
disqualifying conflict of interest are set forth in Section 83107. Included among those interests is an
official's interest in any real property in which the official has an interest of $2,000 or more
(Section 87103(b)) and an interest in the official's personal finances and those of immediate family
members (see Section 87103).

Based on the facts presented, the Mayor has a real property interest in his residence,
assuming he has an interest of $2,000 or more in that real property, and an interest in his personal
finances and those of immediate family members with respect to decisions relating to the Project.

File No. A-18-223
Page No. 4

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in the decision, and that an official's interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. An interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision at issue involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest.

Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the effect of the decision on an official's inexplicitly involved interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

Regulation 18702.2 provides the Act's standards for determining whether a decision's reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's real property interest is material. Under the following relevant provisions, an effect on a real property interest is material if the decision:

(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest;

[*]...[*]

(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of the official's property.

Regulation 18702.2(a)(10): Character of the Real Property

According to the DEIR, if the Project is approved at its maximum 126-unit capacity, it will result in significant impacts to air quality and transportation, circulation, and parking even after mitigation, and significant impacts to noise levels reduced to less than significant by mitigation. The Project Site is currently completely comprised of dedicated open space. The eradication of that open space and introduction of up to 126 additional single-family homes in its place will considerably increase the area surrounding the Project's intensity of use and the traffic going to and from that area.

The Mayor's residence's primary viewsheds are direct away from the EVA and the Project, and the residential portion of the Project will not be visible from the Mayor's residence. However, some grading for the EVA will be visible from the side yard of the Mayor's residence during the EVA's construction, and portions of the completed EVA will also be visible from that perspective. Moreover, grading for and construction of the EVA immediately north of the Mayor's residence

File No. A-18-223
Page No. 5

may temporarily reduce its privacy, and the addition of up to 126 additional residences into the
existing open space less than a half mile east of his residence would permanently affect its privacy.

Thus, it is reasonably foreseeable the decisions relating to the Project would have a material
financial effect on the Mayor's real property interest in his residence under Regulation
18702.2(a)(10).

Regulation 18702.2(a)(12): Market Value of the Real Property

The Mayor's residence is unique because there is dedicated open space north and east of the
property. If the Project is approved, however, those areas would no longer be dedicated open space;
instead, the Mayor's residence would be approximately 882 feet south of the grading for the EVA,
approximately 933 feet south of the completed EVA, and approximately 2,517 feet west of the
residential portion of the Project. The Mayor's residence's loss of surrounding open space due to
the potential approval and construction of the Project could influence its market value.

Many residents of the area surrounding the Project, including the Mayor, rely on Bollinger
Canyon Road and Valley Hill Drive for ingress and egress. The construction of the left-turn lane on
southbound St. Mary's Road at its intersection with Bollinger Canyon Road, the improvement of
Bollinger Canyon Road between Joseph Drive and Valley Hill Drive, and the installation of
underground utilities beneathing Bollinger Canyon Road are likely to cause traffic delays going to and
from the Mayor's residence and could result in temporary closures of those roadways. While these
impacts of the potential approval and construction of the Project would be temporary, they could
also be carried out over a substantial period of time. Furthermore, as noted above, decisions relating
to the Project would substantially alter traffic levels and intensity of use of property surrounding the
Mayor's residence. Thus, the decisions relating to the Project would also have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on the Mayor's real property interest in his residence under
Regulation 18702.2(a)(12). Thus, we conclude that the Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in
those decisions because they would have a disqualifying effect on his real property interest in his
residence.²

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

BGL:jgl

² We do not analyze the decisions relating to the Project's effect on the Mayor's interest in his personal
finances or those of immediate family members because we have already determined that those decisions would have a
disqualifying effect on the Mayor's interest in his real property interest in his residence.

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Mayor Trotter from taking part in governmental decisions relating to 
the Bollinger Valley Project, a residential development project that would allow for up to 126 
single-family homes, given that the Project is located within approximately 2,517 feet of the 
Mayor's residence, and that street construction and improvements necessary for the Project are 
located within approximately 650 feet of his residence?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in those decisions because they would 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest in his residence.

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, participating

assuming he has an interest of $2,000 or more in that real property, and an interest in his personal 
finances and those of immediate family members with respect to decisions relating to the Project. 


Foreseeability and Materiality

substantiaily altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of

financial interest;

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Mayor Trotter from taking part in governmental decisions relating to 
the Bollinger Valley Project, a residential development project that would allow for up to 126 
single-family homes, given that the Project is located within approximately 2,517 feet of the 
Mayor's residence, and that street construction and improvements necessary for the Project are 
located within approximately 650 feet of his residence?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in those decisions because they would 
have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest in his residence.
